title: Cross-Functional Team Mentorship
body: Provided technical mentorship across developers, DBAs, and business analysts throughout 17 years at Durham — not siloed to a single discipline. Established architectural standards and guided skill development through collaborative peer code reviews and technical direction. Operated by influence and example in a unionized environment rather than positional authority.

title: Cross-Divisional Security Modernization
body: Led a cross-divisional team through a structured application portfolio rationalization spanning multiple municipal departments. Coordinated assessment, classification, prioritization, and remediation execution across a large and varied application estate, managing stakeholder expectations, vendor engagements, and internal team workload across the full initiative lifecycle.

title: RFP and Vendor Governance
body: Led RFP and RFQ evaluations for software, hardware, and professional services, including Commvault backup infrastructure and Vena financial planning. Enforced consistent vendor governance standards across all third-party implementations: no SA accounts, group managed service accounts, enterprise backup chain preservation, supervised access during installs, and mandatory patch coordination through change management.

title: Engineering Practice and Standards
body: Introduced TDD, dependency injection, and SOLID principles into Durham's development practice before the acronyms were common currency. Championed source control adoption from VSS through TFS to Azure DevOps, established CI/CD pipelines, and drove the team from ad-hoc deployment toward structured branching strategies and release cycles. Set naming conventions, metadata documentation practices, and data standards that persisted across the organization.

title: Architectural Decision Making
body: Approached architecture as a collaborative problem-solving process. Brought options, trade-off analysis, and working prototypes to the team so decisions were grounded in evidence rather than assumption. When building an enterprise service health dashboard, evaluated Power BI against the core requirement of fast initial load and near-real-time visibility. Power BI's dependency chain and rendering overhead made it the wrong tool for that problem. Chose React with MS Fabric Eventhouse instead, deliberately and for documented reasons. The right answer always depended on the problem, not the preferred tool.
